news_summary: Breakthroughs in gene editing, cartilage regeneration, HIV vaccine development, and enhanced global strategies against fungal diseases highlight significant advances in health and medical research.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées décisives réalisées dans les domaines de l'édition génétique, de la régénération du cartilage, du développement d'un vaccin contre le VIH et du renforcement des stratégies mondiales de lutte contre les mycoses témoignent des progrès significatifs accomplis dans le domaine de la santé et de la recherche médicale.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la edición genética, la regeneración del cartílago, el desarrollo de vacunas contra el VIH y la mejora de las estrategias globales contra las enfermedades fúngicas ponen de relieve los importantes progresos realizados en el ámbito de la salud y la investigación médica.

articles.title: I’m the first person whose life was saved by CRISPR base editing
articles.summary: When standard leukaemia treatments failed, 13-year-old Alyssa Tapley was told she had only weeks left – but then she was offered an experimental procedure
articles.content:
When standard leukaemia treatments failed, 13-year-old Alyssa Tapley was told she had only weeks left – but then she was offered an experimental procedure

Alyssa Tapley received life-saving CRISPR treatment Alyssa Tapley

When the bone marrow transplant failed to treat my leukaemia, it was like: this is it, there’s nothing else now. The doctors were telling my parents it was a matter of weeks. Not years, not months, but weeks.

I’d just turned 13, and I was thinking: “Oh my gosh, this is my last birthday. I’m never gonna grow up and have a family and do all the things that normal people are completely used to, like those normal, everyday things.”

Advertisement

But then we heard about the trial and went down to Great Ormond Street Hospital in London. It was like sci-fi. They were like: “Oh, we’re gonna put some CAR Ts in, and they’re gonna multiply and multiply some more, and go around and fight and kill your all your cancer cells.”

It all started after Easter in 2021. When I went back to school after the coronavirus lockdown ended, I was really tired. I found it hard to walk home from school; I was falling asleep during breaks and lunches. Eventually, I was too sick to go to school.

Really early one morning, my dad noticed my breathing didn’t sound good, and we ended up in A&E. When they put the monitor on me, they started shouting for help. I was in intensive care with double pneumonia for days, and when I woke up, I found out I’d already been put on chemotherapy for leukaemia – that’s when the immune cells in your blood turn cancerous.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

Mum and Dad say it took the doctors a few days to work out what was wrong. That might be because I had T-cell leukaemia, which is less common than B-cell leukaemia.

Then it all moved really quickly. I had a month of chemo at Leicester Royal Infirmary. It wasn’t working, so I was put on more intense chemo, but that didn’t work either. So at the end of October, I went to Sheffield Children’s Hospital for a bone marrow transplant. The idea is, you kill off the blood stem cells, including the cancerous ones, and then you replace them with the transplant.

I was there for five and a half weeks, and I was like, I’m not spending Christmas in hospital, no. And I did get home for Christmas, but afterwards I got a fever and had to go back to Sheffield. That’s when we found out that the transplant hadn’t worked.

There was nothing else the doctors could do. For me, I don’t think it really sunk in at the time. But for Mum and Dad, it was hard. Mum says nothing was harder than not having hope.

She and Dad started looking everywhere, trying to find out if there was anything at all that could work, if we could go to other countries. They were looking at remortgaging the house and things. And they kept on hearing about CAR Ts – that’s when you change T-cells so they kill cancerous cells – and how they can work when bone marrow transplants fail. But they soon realised it only works for B-cell leukaemia, because if you make T-cells attack T-cells, they just kill each other.

Then my consultant from Sheffield heard about a trial Professor Waseem Qasim was organising. He was using CRISPR base editing to change the CAR T-cells so they don’t look like T cells anymore and so don’t kill each other. And my consultant was like, my patient might be a really good fit. That’s when we went down to Great Ormond Street to meet Professor Waseem and Doctor Robert Chiesa, who ran the trial.

Mum and Dad weren’t sure about me doing the trial. They were worried it wouldn’t work and I’d spent my last weeks in hospital in pain, when we could do stuff like going to Disneyland instead. But they let me make the decision.

I was like, I’m doing it. If it’s not gonna help me, it’s gonna help someone else. I was 13 then, I hadn’t really had a chance to do anything, I wasn’t leaving behind anything, I’d had no impact. So it was a way for me to take control of something that I hadn’t been in control of for such a long time, and to try to make a difference for someone else, even if it didn’t work for me.

I had two weeks of conditioning in hospital before I had the CAR T-cells. We had some camera crews film it; it was really amazing. A week later, Dr Chiesa told us they’d multiplied. It was the first sign it was working.

Everybody at Great Ormond Street was so great. I made a friend even though we didn’t meet each other for two months, we just texted, and got the nurses and the play specialists to put stuff on each other’s windows. And I had another friend who was next door to me, but unfortunately she wasn’t able to have a bone marrow transplant, so she passed away.

After four weeks, they tested my bone marrow, and there was nothing there. There were no blood cells to detect. Two weeks later, there was still nothing there, so we went ahead and did my second bone marrow transplant, to replace the blood stem cells.

The hardest part was going home. In the hospital, there was almost always someone around that you could talk to. But then when I went home, I wasn’t allowed out of the house in case I caught something, and I wasn’t allowed to see any of my friends. Mum started going back to work, so it was just me and my dog Holly for almost a year.

I’m still in remission, but not everything is resolved. My thyroid is underactive, but that’s because of all the chemo, not because of the CAR Ts. This is why it’s so important to keep pushing it, so maybe someday people won’t have to have this intensive chemo and can go straight on to have CAR Ts.

I turned 17 in January. I’m doing my A-levels and learning to drive now, which is a bit scary. I want to get a degree apprenticeship in biomedical science. If I can do half as much for someone as the treatment did for me, it would be amazing.

I also get to go to conferences to talk about my experience. At one of them, we met Professor David Liu, who developed base editing. I actually cried when I met him. The video’s quite embarrassing.

I’m so pleased and privileged to have the opportunity to go and do these things, and talk about why science is so important, and why research is so important. Without it, I wouldn’t be here.

As told to Michael Le Page
articles.reasoning: The article reports a life-saving medical breakthrough using CRISPR base editing to treat a rare and fatal form of leukaemia in a young patient. This treatment represents a significant advance in cancer therapy with broad implications for patients worldwide. The story is detailed, focusing on the patient's journey and the innovative treatment, highlighting the real-world positive impact and hope for future patients.
articles.personality_title: 13-year-old’s life saved by pioneering CRISPR treatment for leukaemia
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alyssa Tapley, a 13-year-old girl, was diagnosed with a rare and dangerous type of leukaemia called T-cell leukaemia. Traditional treatments like chemotherapy and bone marrow transplants did not work for her, and doctors said she had only weeks to live.

**What happened** – Alyssa joined an experimental treatment trial at Great Ormond Street Hospital in London. This trial used a new method called CRISPR base editing to change immune cells called CAR T-cells so they could fight her cancer without destroying each other. After receiving this treatment, Alyssa’s cancer cells disappeared, and she later had a second bone marrow transplant to rebuild her healthy blood cells.

**Impact** – This treatment is the first time CRISPR base editing has saved a life in such a case. It shows a new way to treat T-cell leukaemia, which is harder to cure than other types. Alyssa’s story proves that gene-editing technology can help patients who have no other options, offering hope for many others with similar illnesses.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to study this treatment to make it safer and more effective. They hope to reduce the need for harsh chemotherapy and help more patients worldwide. Alyssa is now healthy enough to study and talk about her experience, inspiring others and supporting scientific progress.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new CRISPR gene-editing treatment gave a teenager with untreatable leukaemia a second chance at life, opening doors for future cures.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une adolescente sauvée par un traitement révolutionnaire CRISPR contre la leucémie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Alyssa Tapley, une fille de 13 ans, a été diagnostiquée avec une forme rare et grave de leucémie appelée leucémie T-cellulaire. Les traitements classiques comme la chimiothérapie et la greffe de moelle osseuse n’ont pas fonctionné, et les médecins ont annoncé qu’il ne lui restait que quelques semaines à vivre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Alyssa a rejoint un essai clinique expérimental à l’hôpital Great Ormond Street de Londres. Ce traitement utilisait une nouvelle méthode appelée édition de base CRISPR pour modifier des cellules immunitaires, les cellules CAR T, afin qu’elles combattent son cancer sans s’entre-tuer. Après ce traitement, les cellules cancéreuses d’Alyssa ont disparu, et elle a ensuite reçu une seconde greffe de moelle osseuse pour reconstruire ses cellules sanguines saines.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que l’édition de base CRISPR sauve une vie dans un cas comme celui-ci. Ce traitement montre une nouvelle façon de soigner la leucémie T-cellulaire, plus difficile à guérir que d’autres types. L’histoire d’Alyssa prouve que la technologie d’édition génétique peut aider des patients sans autre option, offrant de l’espoir à beaucoup d’autres.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier ce traitement pour le rendre plus sûr et efficace. Ils espèrent réduire le besoin de chimiothérapies lourdes et aider plus de patients dans le monde. Alyssa est maintenant en bonne santé, elle étudie et partage son expérience pour inspirer les autres et soutenir le progrès scientifique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un traitement innovant d’édition génétique CRISPR a donné une seconde chance à une adolescente atteinte d’une leucémie incurable, ouvrant la voie à de futurs traitements.
articles.personality_title_es: Una adolescente salva su vida gracias a un tratamiento pionero con CRISPR contra la leucemia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Alyssa Tapley, una niña de 13 años, fue diagnosticada con una forma rara y grave de leucemia llamada leucemia de células T. Los tratamientos tradicionales como la quimioterapia y el trasplante de médula ósea no funcionaron, y los médicos le dijeron que le quedaban solo semanas de vida.

**Qué pasó** – Alyssa participó en un ensayo experimental en el hospital Great Ormond Street de Londres. Este tratamiento usó una nueva técnica llamada edición base CRISPR para modificar células inmunitarias llamadas células CAR T, para que lucharan contra su cáncer sin destruirse entre ellas. Después del tratamiento, las células cancerosas desaparecieron y luego recibió un segundo trasplante de médula ósea para reconstruir sus células sanguíneas sanas.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que la edición base CRISPR salva una vida en un caso así. Este tratamiento muestra una nueva forma de tratar la leucemia de células T, que es más difícil de curar que otros tipos. La historia de Alyssa demuestra que la tecnología de edición genética puede ayudar a pacientes sin otras opciones, ofreciendo esperanza a muchos más.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando este tratamiento para hacerlo más seguro y efectivo. Esperan reducir la necesidad de quimioterapia intensa y ayudar a más pacientes en todo el mundo. Alyssa ahora está sana, estudiando y compartiendo su experiencia para inspirar a otros y apoyar el avance científico.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo tratamiento de edición genética CRISPR le dio a una adolescente con leucemia incurable una segunda oportunidad de vida, abriendo puertas para futuras curas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a young, glowing white blood cell shaped like a guardian angel with gentle arms formed from DNA strands, standing protectively over a small, fragile seedling wrapped in a delicate double helix, set against a soft, natural background of intertwining molecular patterns symbolizing CRISPR base editing healing and renewal.

articles.title: How blocking one protein regenerates knee cartilage in aging mice and human tissue
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM A Stanford Medicine study has identified a protein that roughly doubles in aging joints and blocks cartilage from repairing itself. Blocking that protein in older mice regenerated hyaline cartilage across the joint surface. Human tissue samples from knee replacement surgeries responded the same way after just one week of […]
The post How blocking one protein regenerates knee cartilage in aging mice and human tissue first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

A Stanford Medicine study has identified a protein that roughly doubles in aging joints and blocks cartilage from repairing itself. Blocking that protein in older mice regenerated hyaline cartilage across the joint surface. Human tissue samples from knee replacement surgeries responded the same way after just one week of treatment.

The protein, 15-PGDH, is a gerozyme: a class of proteins that accumulate with age and suppress tissue regeneration across the body. It works by degrading prostaglandin E2, a molecule that drives tissue repair. As 15-PGDH rises with age, prostaglandin E2 falls, and regeneration slows across muscle, bone, nerve, and blood. The same team identified gerozymes in 2023. This paper, published in Science, is the first to show the mechanism in cartilage.

How cartilage aging differs from other tissues

Cartilage regeneration does not work the way researchers expected. Most tissue repair runs on stem cells: they multiply, differentiate, and produce new specialized cells. Cartilage appears to skip that entirely.

The cells already in the joint, called chondrocytes, can shift their gene expression and return to a more functional state without any stem cell involvement. “We were looking for stem cells, but they are clearly not involved,” said Helen Blau, PhD, professor of microbiology and immunology and senior author of the study. “It’s very exciting.”

In aging joints, chondrocytes drift toward inflammation and collagen breakdown. As collagen goes, cartilage thins and softens, producing the pain and swelling of osteoarthritis. Elevated 15-PGDH is part of what pushes cells in that direction.

What blocking 15-PGDH did in older mice

Researchers gave older mice a small molecule that blocks 15-PGDH, delivered either by abdominal injection or directly into the knee joint. Both worked. Cartilage that had thinned with age grew thicker, and the new tissue was hyaline cartilage, the smooth, load-bearing type needed for normal joint movement, not fibrocartilage, a lower-quality substitute.

In untreated aging cartilage, chondrocytes responsible for breakdown made up eight percent of cells; after treatment, three percent. Cells associated with fibrocartilage production dropped from 16 to 8 percent. Cells that build hyaline cartilage and maintain the surrounding matrix rose from 22 to 42 percent.

“Cartilage regeneration to such an extent in aged mice took us by surprise,” said Nidhi Bhutani, PhD, associate professor of orthopedic surgery and co-senior author. “The effect was remarkable.”

Protection after ACL-type injury

About half of people who tear their ACL develop osteoarthritis in that joint within 15 years. In the mouse model, untreated ACL-injured animals had 15-PGDH levels roughly twice those of uninjured mice and had osteoarthritis within four weeks. That’s a compressed version of a process that takes over a decade in humans.

Mice given the inhibitor twice weekly for four weeks after injury were far less likely to develop it. They also walked more normally and put more weight on the injured limb.

Human tissue also responded

The most striking result may be the human tissue data. The team tested cartilage from total knee replacement surgeries: end-stage, already-failed joints. After one week of treatment, those samples showed fewer degraded cells, less gene activity tied to breakdown, and new articular cartilage growth.

“It’s clear that a large pool of already existing cells in cartilage are changing their gene expression patterns,” Bhutani said. “And by targeting these cells for regeneration, we may have an opportunity to have a bigger overall impact clinically.”

Toward clinical trials

A Phase 1 trial of a 15-PGDH inhibitor for muscle weakness has already been completed with safety confirmed and biological activity observed in healthy volunteers. A cartilage trial is the next step.

Osteoarthritis affects hundreds of millions of people globally and is a primary driver of joint replacement surgery. No approved drug currently addresses the cartilage loss itself, only the symptoms. “Until now, there has been no drug that directly treats the cause of cartilage loss,” Bhutani said. “But this gerozyme inhibitor causes a dramatic regeneration of cartilage beyond that reported in response to any other drug or intervention.”

Source study: Science— Inhibition of 15-hydroxy prostaglandin dehydrogenase promotes cartilage regeneration

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant biomedical breakthrough where blocking a specific protein regenerates knee cartilage in aging mice and human tissue, potentially addressing osteoarthritis, a widespread condition affecting hundreds of millions globally. The study is detailed, shows clear real-world impact, and points toward clinical trials, indicating broad and meaningful benefits for public health.
articles.personality_title: Blocking one protein helps regrow knee cartilage in aging mice and human tissue
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As people and animals get older, their knee cartilage often breaks down, causing pain and stiffness in joints. This is a big problem called osteoarthritis, which affects millions of people worldwide. So far, no medicine can fix the damaged cartilage itself.

**What happened** – Scientists at Stanford found a protein called 15-PGDH that increases in aging joints and stops cartilage from healing. They tested a treatment that blocks this protein in older mice and in human knee tissue from surgeries. After just one week, the cartilage started to grow back, becoming the smooth kind needed for healthy joints.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that cartilage cells can repair themselves without stem cells, which was unexpected. The treated mice had thicker cartilage and fewer cells that cause damage. The human tissue also showed signs of healing. This could lead to new medicines that actually restore cartilage, rather than just easing pain.

**What's next step** – A safe drug that blocks 15-PGDH has already been tested for muscle problems, and now researchers plan to start clinical trials to see if it can help people with cartilage damage. If successful, this could reduce the need for knee replacement surgeries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Blocking the protein 15-PGDH can regenerate knee cartilage in aging joints, opening the door to new treatments for osteoarthritis.
articles.personality_title_fr: Bloquer une protéine aide à régénérer le cartilage du genou chez la souris âgée et le tissu humain
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Avec l’âge, le cartilage du genou s’use souvent, causant douleur et raideur. Cette maladie, appelée arthrose, touche des millions de personnes dans le monde. Jusqu’à présent, aucun médicament ne pouvait réparer ce cartilage endommagé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Stanford ont découvert une protéine nommée 15-PGDH qui augmente dans les articulations vieillissantes et empêche la réparation du cartilage. Ils ont testé un traitement qui bloque cette protéine chez des souris âgées et sur du tissu humain issu d’opérations du genou. Après seulement une semaine, le cartilage a commencé à repousser, redevenant lisse et fonctionnel.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que les cellules du cartilage peuvent se réparer elles-mêmes sans faire appel aux cellules souches, ce qui était inattendu. Les souris traitées avaient un cartilage plus épais et moins de cellules nuisibles. Le tissu humain montrait aussi des signes de guérison. Cela pourrait mener à des médicaments qui restaurent réellement le cartilage, au lieu de seulement soulager la douleur.

**Prochaine étape** – Un médicament bloquant la 15-PGDH a déjà été testé en sécurité pour des problèmes musculaires, et les chercheurs prévoient maintenant des essais cliniques pour vérifier son efficacité sur le cartilage humain. Si tout se passe bien, cela pourrait diminuer le nombre d’opérations de remplacement du genou.

**Phrase clé** – Bloquer la protéine 15-PGDH peut régénérer le cartilage du genou chez les personnes âgées, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements contre l’arthrose.
articles.personality_title_es: Bloquear una proteína ayuda a regenerar el cartílago de la rodilla en ratones viejos y tejido humano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que las personas y animales envejecen, el cartílago de sus rodillas suele desgastarse, causando dolor y rigidez. Esta condición, llamada osteoartritis, afecta a millones en todo el mundo. Hasta ahora, no existía un medicamento que pudiera reparar el cartílago dañado.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de Stanford identificaron una proteína llamada 15-PGDH que aumenta en las articulaciones envejecidas y bloquea la reparación del cartílago. Probaron un tratamiento que bloquea esta proteína en ratones viejos y en tejido humano de cirugías de rodilla. Después de solo una semana, el cartílago comenzó a crecer de nuevo, volviéndose suave y funcional.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra que las células del cartílago pueden repararse sin usar células madre, algo inesperado. Los ratones tratados tuvieron cartílago más grueso y menos células dañinas. El tejido humano también mostró señales de curación. Esto podría llevar a nuevos medicamentos que realmente restauren el cartílago, en lugar de solo aliviar el dolor.

**Próximo paso** – Un medicamento que bloquea la 15-PGDH ya fue probado con seguridad para problemas musculares, y ahora los investigadores planean ensayos clínicos para ver si ayuda a personas con daño en el cartílago. Si funciona, podría reducir la necesidad de cirugías de reemplazo de rodilla.

**Frase clave** – Bloquear la proteína 15-PGDH puede regenerar el cartílago de la rodilla en articulaciones envejecidas, abriendo la puerta a nuevos tratamientos para la osteoartritis.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an aged, weathered knee joint symbolized as a glowing, gently mended bridge made of smooth, translucent cartilage arches, with small luminous chondrocyte-like orbs shifting from dull and fragmented on one side to vibrant and whole on the other, surrounded by subtle, flowing molecular strands representing the blocked 15-PGDH protein dissolving away, all rendered in soft natural earth tones and muted blues.

articles.title: Vaccination elicits HIV broadly neutralizing antibodies in primates
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 30 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10837-5Vaccination elicits HIV broadly neutralizing antibodies in primates
articles.content: The high antigenic diversity of HIV has been a major obstacle to development of a broadly protective vaccine. Nevertheless, protective HIV broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs) exist and have been proposed as templates for vaccine development1-6. Germline-targeting is a conceptually radical vaccine design approach to elicit bnAbs, aiming to prime rare bnAb-precursor B cells possessing pre-determined human genetic and structural features shared with template bnAbs, and then guide B cell affinity maturation to potent bnAb evolution with heterologous boosters7-11. Although the approach has shown promise in clinical12-17 and pre-clinical18-34 studies, it faces many immunological challenges and, to date, has not succeeded in generating bnAbs in humans or nontransgenic animals. Here, we report an adjuvanted protein germline-targeting vaccine tested in outbred nonhuman primates that generated bnAb-class memory B cells and sera capable of neutralizing diverse HIV clinical isolates. bnAb lineages were generated in ≥50% of animals, achieving up to 67% neutralization breadth compared to the reference bnAb. Vaccine-induced bnAbs exhibited precise structural mimicry of human bnAb interactions with HIV envelope (Env), matching the germline-targeting predictions. Furthermore, serum bnAb activity developed in 44% of animals and in the most striking instance reached titers expected to confer protection against diverse HIV isolates. These results demonstrate proof of principle that germline-targeting vaccines can reproducibly elicit prespecified classes of bnAbs to prespecified epitopes under endogenous conditions, supporting further optimization of this approach for HIV vaccine development.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in HIV vaccine development, demonstrating for the first time that a germline-targeting vaccine can elicit broadly neutralizing antibodies in primates. This has broad implications for HIV prevention globally, addressing a major health challenge with a novel and promising approach.
articles.personality_title: New HIV vaccine approach produces strong antibodies in primates
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – HIV is a virus that changes a lot, making it very hard to create a vaccine that protects against all its types. Scientists have found special antibodies called broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs) that can fight many HIV strains, but so far, vaccines have not been able to make the body produce these bnAbs in humans or animals.

**What happened** – A team of researchers tested a new vaccine on primates (monkeys) that uses a method called germline-targeting. This vaccine is designed to start the production of bnAbs by activating specific immune cells. The vaccine successfully caused more than half of the monkeys to produce bnAbs that could stop many types of HIV. In some cases, the antibodies were strong enough to provide protection against different HIV strains.

**Impact** – This is the first time a vaccine has shown it can reliably create these powerful antibodies in primates without genetic modifications. The antibodies made were very similar to the ones found in humans who can naturally fight HIV. This success means scientists have a clearer path to developing an effective HIV vaccine, which has been a goal for many years.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to improve this vaccine approach and test it further, including in human trials. They want to make sure it is safe, effective, and can protect people from HIV in the real world.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new vaccine method has successfully triggered strong, broad HIV-fighting antibodies in primates, marking a major step toward an effective HIV vaccine.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle approche vaccinale contre le VIH produit de puissants anticorps chez les primates
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le VIH est un virus qui change beaucoup, ce qui rend très difficile la création d’un vaccin protégeant contre toutes ses formes. Les scientifiques ont découvert des anticorps spéciaux appelés anticorps neutralisants à large spectre (bnAbs) capables de combattre de nombreux types de VIH, mais jusqu’à présent, les vaccins n’ont pas réussi à faire produire ces bnAbs chez l’homme ou les animaux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs a testé un nouveau vaccin sur des primates (singes) utilisant une méthode appelée ciblage des gènes germinaux. Ce vaccin est conçu pour démarrer la production de bnAbs en activant des cellules immunitaires spécifiques. Le vaccin a réussi à faire produire des bnAbs à plus de la moitié des singes, capables d’arrêter de nombreux types de VIH. Dans certains cas, les anticorps étaient assez forts pour offrir une protection contre différentes souches de VIH.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un vaccin montre qu’il peut créer de manière fiable ces anticorps puissants chez des primates sans modifications génétiques. Les anticorps produits étaient très similaires à ceux trouvés chez des humains capables de combattre naturellement le VIH. Ce succès offre une nouvelle voie claire pour développer un vaccin efficace contre le VIH, un objectif de longue date.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer cette approche vaccinale et à la tester davantage, y compris chez l’humain. Ils veulent s’assurer qu’elle est sûre, efficace et capable de protéger les personnes contre le VIH dans la vie réelle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode vaccinale a déclenché avec succès de puissants anticorps capables de combattre largement le VIH chez les primates, marquant une avancée majeure vers un vaccin efficace contre le VIH.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método de vacuna contra el VIH produce anticuerpos potentes en primates
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El VIH es un virus que cambia mucho, lo que hace muy difícil crear una vacuna que proteja contra todos sus tipos. Los científicos han encontrado anticuerpos especiales llamados anticuerpos neutralizantes ampliamente (bnAbs) que pueden combatir muchas variedades de VIH, pero hasta ahora las vacunas no habían logrado que el cuerpo produzca estos bnAbs en humanos o animales.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores probó una nueva vacuna en primates (monos) que usa un método llamado apuntar a la línea germinal. Esta vacuna está diseñada para iniciar la producción de bnAbs activando células inmunes específicas. La vacuna logró que más de la mitad de los monos produjeran bnAbs capaces de detener muchos tipos de VIH. En algunos casos, los anticuerpos fueron lo suficientemente fuertes para proteger contra diferentes cepas de VIH.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que una vacuna muestra que puede crear de forma confiable estos poderosos anticuerpos en primates sin modificaciones genéticas. Los anticuerpos producidos fueron muy similares a los encontrados en humanos que pueden combatir el VIH naturalmente. Este éxito significa que los científicos tienen un camino más claro para desarrollar una vacuna eficaz contra el VIH, un objetivo que ha durado muchos años.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán mejorando este enfoque de vacuna y lo probarán más, incluso en ensayos con humanos. Quieren asegurarse de que sea seguro, efectivo y pueda proteger a las personas del VIH en la vida real.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo método de vacuna ha logrado activar con éxito anticuerpos fuertes y amplios contra el VIH en primates, marcando un paso importante hacia una vacuna efectiva contra el VIH.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a diverse group of elegant primates gathered around a glowing, intricately patterned shield symbolizing the HIV virus envelope, with delicate golden threads weaving from the primates’ hearts to the shield, representing the generation of broadly neutralizing antibodies, all set against a soft, natural background of intertwined DNA helixes and abstract molecular structures.

articles.title: New WHO plan urges stronger action against fungal disease
articles.summary: The World Health Organization (WHO) has published new guidance to help countries confront the rising burden of fungal disease and antifungal resistance - one of the most neglected global health threats, yet still largely absent from national health plans.
articles.content:
The new Blueprint for strengthening responses to fungal disease and antifungal resistance, issued on Tuesday, sets out practical steps to improve prevention, diagnosis, treatment and surveillance.

Fungal diseases affect more than 300 million people each year and are associated with high mortality, long-term illness and major losses in health and productivity worldwide.

Growing global threat

They range from common conditions such as ringworm and nail infections to severe invasive diseases that can be deadly, especially for people with weakened immune systems, those receiving intensive care, people living with HIV, transplant recipients and cancer patients.

Meanwhile, antifungal resistance is a growing threat, driven in part by the widespread use of antifungal medications and their analogues across human, animal and plant health, as well as environmental exposure to antifungal chemicals.

Despite this toll, WHO said fungal diseases are often missing from national health treatment policies, global burden-of-disease estimates and most strategies on antimicrobial resistance (AMR), universal health coverage and One Health – the UN agency’s initiative for action across human, animal, plant and environmental health.

‘A concrete path forward’

The blueprint comes roughly a month after WHO’s decision-making body, the World Health Assembly, adopted an updated Global Action Plan on AMR which occurs when bacteria, viruses, fungi and parasites evolve and become resistant to the drugs designed to kill them, making infections harder to treat.

It remains one of the top global health and development threats.

“The Updated Global Action Plan on AMR approved by the 79th World Health Assembly recognised that antifungal resistance is an integral part of the AMR challenge – and one we can no longer afford to overlook,” said Dr Jean Pierre Nyemazi, interim Director of WHO’s Department of Antimicrobial Resistance.

He added that the Blueprint “gives countries a concrete path forward.”

Addressing critical gaps

The Blueprint builds on WHO’s first Fungal Priority Pathogens List, published in 2022, which identified 19 fungal pathogens or pathogen groups requiring urgent research, development and public health action.

It was developed through a multi-stage process and consultations with more than 150 experts from all WHO regions, including specialists in clinical mycology, diagnostics, stewardship, surveillance, regulatory policy, public health and patient advocacy.

The aim is to help countries address critical gaps in knowledge, diagnosis, treatment, surveillance, research, and workforce capacities, particularly in low-resource settings.

Practical framework for response

The WHO blueprint prioritizes interventions around four interlinked domains, providing a framework for implementation:

Domain 1 focuses on public health and health systems, including strengthening awareness and readiness, antifungal stewardship programmes, workforce training, and infection prevention and control.

Domain 2 concerns expanding equitable access to quality-assured antifungal medicines and diagnostics, while supporting research, innovation, and market.

Domain 3 prioritises strengthening laboratory systems and surveillance to support clinical management, inform public health decision-making and enhance outbreak preparedness

Domain 4 addresses social and environmental drivers, including agricultural, environmental and One Health factors that can contribute to fungal disease epidemiology and antifungal resistance.

“Fungal disease and antifungal resistance remain an under addressed priority across national health plans, AMR strategies, and surveillance systems.

“This blueprint provides countries with a practical framework to strengthen their response,” said Hatim Sati, Technical Officer in WHO’s Department of Antimicrobial Resistance, who led the development of the guidance.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant new WHO plan to combat fungal diseases and antifungal resistance, which affect hundreds of millions globally and pose a serious health threat. The plan provides a comprehensive, practical framework for countries to improve prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and surveillance, addressing a neglected but critical global health issue with broad impact.
articles.personality_title: WHO releases new plan to fight fungal diseases and drug resistance
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Fungal diseases affect over 300 million people worldwide each year. These illnesses range from common infections like ringworm to deadly conditions, especially for people with weak immune systems. At the same time, fungi are becoming resistant to medicines, making infections harder to treat. Despite this, many countries have not included fungal diseases in their health plans.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, the World Health Organization (WHO) introduced a new Blueprint to help countries better prevent, diagnose, treat, and track fungal diseases and resistance to antifungal drugs. This plan follows an updated global action plan on antimicrobial resistance (AMR), which includes resistance in fungi, bacteria, viruses, and parasites.

**Impact** – The Blueprint is important because it offers clear steps to improve health systems, access to medicines, lab testing, and understanding of environmental factors that affect fungal diseases. It was created with input from over 150 experts worldwide and focuses on four main areas: public health systems, access to medicines and tests, laboratory and surveillance improvements, and environmental and social causes. This makes it easier for countries, especially those with fewer resources, to act against a serious but often overlooked health problem.

**What's next step** – Countries are expected to use the WHO Blueprint to update their health policies and programs. This could lead to better training for healthcare workers, improved availability of antifungal medicines, stronger lab networks to detect infections early, and more attention to how farming and the environment contribute to fungal disease and resistance.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The WHO’s new Blueprint gives countries practical guidance to fight fungal diseases and drug resistance, aiming to save lives and improve health worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’OMS publie un nouveau plan pour lutter contre les maladies fongiques et la résistance aux médicaments
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les maladies fongiques touchent chaque année plus de 300 millions de personnes dans le monde. Ces maladies vont d’infections courantes comme la teigne à des affections mortelles, surtout chez les personnes dont le système immunitaire est affaibli. En même temps, les champignons développent une résistance aux médicaments, rendant les infections plus difficiles à soigner. Pourtant, beaucoup de pays n’ont pas inclus ces maladies dans leurs plans de santé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mardi, l’Organisation mondiale de la santé (OMS) a présenté un nouveau plan, appelé « Blueprint », pour aider les pays à mieux prévenir, diagnostiquer, traiter et surveiller les maladies fongiques et la résistance aux antifongiques. Ce plan suit une mise à jour du plan mondial contre la résistance aux antimicrobiens (RAM), qui inclut la résistance des champignons, bactéries, virus et parasites.

**Impact** – Ce Blueprint est important car il propose des étapes claires pour améliorer les systèmes de santé, l’accès aux médicaments, les tests en laboratoire et la compréhension des facteurs environnementaux liés aux maladies fongiques. Il a été créé avec la participation de plus de 150 experts dans le monde et se concentre sur quatre domaines : les systèmes de santé publique, l’accès aux médicaments et diagnostics, l’amélioration des laboratoires et de la surveillance, ainsi que les causes sociales et environnementales. Cela facilite l’action des pays, surtout ceux aux ressources limitées, face à un problème de santé grave souvent négligé.

**Prochaine étape** – Les pays devraient utiliser ce Blueprint pour mettre à jour leurs politiques et programmes de santé. Cela pourrait entraîner une meilleure formation des professionnels de santé, une meilleure disponibilité des médicaments antifongiques, des réseaux de laboratoires renforcés pour détecter rapidement les infections, et plus d’attention aux liens entre agriculture, environnement et maladies fongiques.

**Phrase clé** – Le nouveau Blueprint de l’OMS offre aux pays des conseils pratiques pour lutter contre les maladies fongiques et la résistance aux médicaments, dans le but de sauver des vies et d’améliorer la santé dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: La OMS lanza un nuevo plan para combatir las enfermedades fúngicas y la resistencia a medicamentos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las enfermedades fúngicas afectan a más de 300 millones de personas en todo el mundo cada año. Estas enfermedades van desde infecciones comunes como la tiña hasta condiciones mortales, especialmente para personas con sistemas inmunitarios débiles. Al mismo tiempo, los hongos están desarrollando resistencia a los medicamentos, lo que dificulta el tratamiento. Sin embargo, muchos países no han incluido estas enfermedades en sus planes de salud.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, la Organización Mundial de la Salud (OMS) presentó un nuevo Plan, llamado Blueprint, para ayudar a los países a mejorar la prevención, el diagnóstico, el tratamiento y el seguimiento de las enfermedades fúngicas y la resistencia a los antifúngicos. Este plan sigue a una actualización del Plan Global contra la Resistencia a los Antimicrobianos (RAM), que incluye la resistencia en hongos, bacterias, virus y parásitos.

**Impacto** – El Blueprint es importante porque ofrece pasos claros para mejorar los sistemas de salud, el acceso a medicamentos, las pruebas de laboratorio y la comprensión de los factores ambientales que afectan las enfermedades fúngicas. Fue creado con la participación de más de 150 expertos de todo el mundo y se centra en cuatro áreas principales: sistemas de salud pública, acceso a medicamentos y pruebas, mejoras en laboratorios y vigilancia, y causas sociales y ambientales. Esto facilita que los países, especialmente los con menos recursos, actúen contra un problema grave pero a menudo olvidado.

**Próximo paso** – Se espera que los países usen el Blueprint de la OMS para actualizar sus políticas y programas de salud. Esto podría llevar a una mejor capacitación del personal médico, mayor disponibilidad de medicamentos antifúngicos, redes de laboratorio más fuertes para detectar infecciones temprano y más atención a cómo la agricultura y el medio ambiente contribuyen a las enfermedades fúngicas y la resistencia.

**Frase clave** – El nuevo Blueprint de la OMS ofrece a los países una guía práctica para combatir las enfermedades fúngicas y la resistencia a medicamentos, con el objetivo de salvar vidas y mejorar la salud en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a protective shield made of intertwined fungal hyphae gently encircling a diverse group of symbolic vessels representing human, animal, plant, and environmental health, with subtle glowing threads connecting them to illustrate a unified, collaborative response against fungal disease and antifungal resistance.
